summary: alya-baileys hijacks installer's authenticated WhatsApp session via remote-controlled config
details: alya-baileys is a fork of the Baileys WhatsApp library that silently weaponizes the installer's authenticated WhatsApp account. On every `connection.open` event, lib/Socket/newsletter.js decrypts a hardcoded AES-256-CBC-encrypted string into a URL pointing at raw.githubusercontent.com and fetches a remote config that controls which newsletter JIDs the installer's WhatsApp account auto-follows, auto-reacts to (with attacker-supplied emoji/keyword maps), and auto-votes on. The config is attacker-mutable at any time, giving the maintainer ongoing remote control over any WhatsApp identity that uses this library to build a bot. The hijack URL and target JID lists are concealed by a custom AES-CBC routine in lib/Utils/alya.js (`alyaDecryptCore`, key derived from sha512('Alyabaileys_alyamoon') reconstructed byte-by-byte to defeat grep, IV 'alyamoonalyamoon'), with junk aliases `hapus_aja` / `gak_penting_asli` ("just delete" / "not important really" in Indonesian) sprinkled across the source and a taunt comment `// You'll never find what this does` left in newsletter.js. Additionally, `fetchLatestBaileysVersion` in lib/Utils/generics.js is repointed from the legitimate Baileys version manifest to `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/diszx-creator/baileys-version/refs/heads/main/baileys-version.json`, an attacker-controlled repo. The package replicates the `makeWASocket` API surface of @whiskeysockets/baileys to attract developers building WhatsApp bots; those installers' WhatsApp identities are then used to amplify attacker-chosen newsletters, reactions, and poll votes without their knowledge.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
